content.full_text:
Farn Pourrclr Pucrrcns CovrvrrssroN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street . Sacramenro, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
July 12,2000
Greg Smith
3 Osprey
Irvine, California 92604
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-00-147
Dear Mr. Smith:
You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").r Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your letter as a
request for informal assistance.2 Please note that this advice is limited to the Act. The
Commission has no authority to provide advice on local restrictions and prohibitions which may
affect you.
QUESTIONS
1. Under what conditions would the transfer or loan of a mailing list from a local
initiative committee to your mayoral committee be considered legal?
2. Under what conditions would such a transfer or loan be considered illegal?
3. May the transfer or loan of the mailing list be made at no cost to the mayoral
committee?
4. Must the transfer or loan of the mailing list be made available to all candidates for
mayor? Must the same terms be offered to all candidates for mayor?
' Gou"--ent Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, California Code
of Regulations Section 18000, et seq.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83 I l4; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


Our File No. I-00-147
Page2
CONCLUSIONS
1. Except as noted below, the Act does not prohibit your committee from receiving a
mailing list from a local initiative committee. However, by providing the mailing list, the
initiative committee would be making an in-kind contribution to your committee, or in the case
of a loan, an in-kind loan to your committee, either of which could be subject to local
contribution limits if such an ordinance exists in your jurisdiction, unless your committee fully
compensates the initiative committee for the mailing list. Any such transaction, whether or not
the fair market value of the mailing list is paid to the initiative committee, must be reported in a
manner consistent with the requirements of the Act.
2. Under the Act's transfer ban, you would be prohibited from receiving contributions
from other candidates' or officeholders' controlled committees if the office you are seeking is
subject to valid contribution limits. Therefore, if the initiative committee is controlled by a
candidate or an offlceholder and you are running for office in a jurisdiction which imposes
contribution limits or are running in a special election to fill a vacant office, you may not accept
the mailing list unless the fair market value of the mailing list is paid to the initiative committee
3. See number 1
4. There is no requirement in the Act that the transfer or loan of a mailing list be made
available to all candidates for mayor, nor is there a requirement that the same terms be offered to
all candidates for mayor.
FACTS
You are an elected member of the city council. You are presently contemplating running
for mayor, for which you will establish a new campaign committee. In late 1999, an initiative
committee was formed in Orange County for the purpose of advocating a "yes" position on an
initiative which appeared on the March, 2000 ballot. The initiative committee solicited
donations and built an extensive mailing list from its contributors. The committee is still actively
raising funds and advertising.
The initiative committee is considering allowing its mailing list to be used by your
mayoral committee for the purpose of advocating your election and raising funds.


Our File No. I-00-147
Page 3
ANALYSIS
In general, the Act does not prohibit your mayoral committee from receiving a mailing
list from an initiative committee. However, by receiving the mailing list, your committee would
be receiving an in-kind contribution (or an in-kind loan if your committee plans to pay the fair
market value of the mailing list to the initiative committee on a later date). The Act defines
"contribution" in terms of "payment," which is in turn defined as, ,rmong other things, "...o
transfer...of...anything of value, whether tangible or intangible." (Sections 82015 and82044.)
We have previously advised that the provision of an organization's mailing list constitutes an in-
kind contribution to the recipient. (McFarlane Advice Letter, No. T-83-065.) The amount of the
contribution is the fair market value of the list, which is the amount it would cost the committee
to purchase a similar list on the open market (e.g., from a mailing house).
No mention was made in your letter whether the initiative committee is controlled by a
candidate or an officeholder. If it is controlled by a candidate or an officeholder, and the city
imposes limits on contributions to candidates, the transfer ban of Proposition 73 would prohibit
the in-kind contribution from the initiative committee. PropositionT3, which was passed by the
voters in June, 1988, added contribution limits to the Act. (Sections 85301-85303.) In addition,
Section 85304 was enacted prohibiting the transfer of campaign funds between candidates.
However, in Servtce Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, CLC v. Fair Political Practices
Commission (9'h Cir. 1992) 955 F. 2d 1312,1322, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals invalidated
the Act's contribution limits insofar as the limits were calculated on a fiscal year basis. The
Court did not invalidate Proposition 73's special election limits, as they are based on an election
cycle rather than a fiscal year. The result of the Court's decision was that Section 85304's inter-
candidate transfer prohibition was also invalidated with regard to fiscal year contribution limits,
but not with regard to limits based on an election cycle. Therefore, transfers between candidate
committees are prohibited when the candidate who will receive the transfer (contribution) is
running in a special election or is subject to local jurisdiction contribution limits which are based
on an election cycle. We advise you to check with your local filing officer (in the event of a city
election, your filing officer is the city clerk) for information and interpretation of any local limits
on contributions or other restrictions that may be in effect in your jurisdiction.
Provided the initiative committee is not controlled by a candidate or an offrceholder, and
you are not subject to local contribution limits, nothing in the Act prohibits your committee from
receiving a mailing list from a local initiative committee. Any such transaction, whether or not
the fair market value of the mailing list is paid to the initiative committee, must be reported in
manner consistent with the requirements of the Act.
If you have any questions regarding this letter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
,I1/,oh
By: Trish Mayer
Political Reform Consultant
